confirmations.reason: Workspace Rules are stored in .agents/rules at the workspace or git root, directly providing a project-scoped instruction file that gives the agent standing instructions and context for that repo.

Plugins are described as namespaced bundles grouping skills, rules, MCP servers, and hooks into a single package — directly matching the capability of bundling reusable capabilities/instructions the agent can load.
Lifecycle Hooks explicitly run custom local shell scripts or commands at specific lifecycle points during execution, directly realizing the capability of running custom logic on agent events.

Rules provide persistent, reusable constraints/context but do not directly shape system prompt, persona, or output style. Rules are constraint/instruction mechanisms, not system-prompt or persona customization surfaces — the capability is specifically about output and system-prompt shaping.
MCP Integration directly implements the Model Context Protocol, which is the standard way to connect external tools, data, and services — an exact match.

The MCP Store explicitly supports browsing, installing, and authenticating MCP servers directly in the editor, which covers the install, configure, and discover aspects of MCP server management and registry.
Per-Tool MCP Permission grants access to a single named tool on a specific MCP server, directly realizing fine-grained allow-listing control over which MCP tools the agent may use.
The Subagents feature describes delegation of long-running operations to parallel autonomous agents, directly matching the capability of spawning focused sub-agents to handle parts of a task.
Multi-Agent Orchestration describes coordinating multiple agents with error recovery, retries, and coordination logic, directly realizing multi-agent teams that share work and communicate.
Workflows are saved markdown files defining a repeatable series of steps to guide the Agent through tasks across sessions — a direct match for defining repeatable, named multi-step task sequences.

Request Review Mode pauses at milestones for user approval but does not produce a reviewable plan before any edits occur. Plan-then-execute requires generating a plan upfront before action; milestone-gated approval is an adjacent but distinct pattern.
Worktree Support explicitly has agents operate in dedicated Git worktrees to isolate parallel work within a project, directly realizing parallel isolated workspaces without collisions.
Background Tasks explicitly run tracked, long-running operations asynchronously without blocking the primary agent — a direct match for background/async execution.
Scheduled Tasks allow users to schedule messages to agents with repeatable time-based triggers, directly realizing the capability of running the agent on a schedule or trigger without supervision.
Terminal Sandboxing provides kernel-level/native OS isolation for terminal commands executed by the Agent, directly realizing sandboxed execution to limit blast radius.
The Fine-Grained Permissions Engine represents every sensitive agent operation as a permission, directly realizing the capability of approving/denying what the agent is allowed to do.

Independent Network Access Control explicitly controls network connectivity for sandboxed commands independently of filesystem restrictions, directly realizing network/egress control for agent actions.
Inspect Hooks are read-only, non-blocking hooks explicitly designed for logging, audit trails, and metrics collection — a direct realization of audit logging/telemetry.
Observability tracks per-turn and cumulative token usage and provides access to thinking traces, directly realizing cost and usage tracking of token spend.

While the MCP Store mentions authentication as one of its features, it is primarily a browsing/installation surface. Authentication to MCP servers (OAuth, tokens) is a distinct protocol-level capability that the Store only partially and incidentally touches — the Store is not itself an authentication mechanism.
